title: Information Theory for Journalists
description: Master the principles of information theory and apply them to journalistic practice through exploration of entropy, channel capacity, and compression.

nodes.title: What Journalists Need to Know About Information
nodes.content: Information theory, developed by Claude Shannon in 1948, provides a mathematical framework for understanding how information is transmitted, stored, and processed. For journalists, this theory explains why some stories are more impactful than others, how to communicate clearly despite interference (misinformation, technical noise), and how to make stories concise without losing meaning. This course explores three core concepts: entropy (measuring information content), channel capacity (maximizing signal despite noise), and compression (efficient storytelling). Understanding these principles will make you a more effective communicator.

nodes.title: Choose Your Path: Which Concept First?
nodes.content: You'll explore three key areas of information theory. Each focuses on a different challenge journalists face. Select which topics interest you most—all paths will eventually converge so you can integrate everything you've learned.

choices.label: Entropy & News Stories: How to measure information impact
Channel Capacity & Signal: How to cut through the noise
Compression & Redundancy: How to tell efficient stories

nodes.title: Entropy: Measuring the Information in Your Story
nodes.content: Entropy is a measure of uncertainty or surprise in a message. In information theory, high-entropy messages carry more information because they're less predictable. For journalists, this means: a story about an unprecedented political event has high entropy (we didn't expect it), while a routine traffic report has low entropy (we expect traffic). The formula H(X) = -Σ p(x) log₂ p(x) quantifies this mathematically. A well-written news story balances entropy—too much randomness becomes confusing, too little becomes boring. Understanding entropy helps you gauge whether your lead is compelling and whether you're burying important information.

nodes.title: Entropy in Action: The Breaking News Story
nodes.content: Consider two headlines about an election: (A) 'Local Official Re-elected with 52% of Vote' has low entropy—elections are frequent, re-elections are common, the margin is typical. (B) 'Unknown Challenger Defeats 12-Term Incumbent with Grassroots Campaign' has high entropy—this outcome is surprising and rare. Headline B conveys more information because it violates reader expectations. When you write, placing high-entropy facts early (the unexpected element) makes your story more informative and engaging. Notice how the second headline tells us something we couldn't have easily predicted beforehand—that's the signal of information theory in action.

nodes.title: Channel Capacity & Signal-to-Noise Ratio
nodes.content: A communication channel has a maximum capacity—the most information it can reliably transmit. In Shannon's theorem, channel capacity C = B × log₂(1 + S/N), where B is bandwidth and S/N is the signal-to-noise ratio. For journalists, the 'channel' is your audience's attention, the 'signal' is your story, and 'noise' includes distractions, misinformation, competing headlines, and platform algorithms. To maximize impact, you must increase signal (clarity, relevance, evidence) and decrease noise (avoiding jargon, correcting falsehoods, removing distractions). Social media algorithms create low-capacity channels by fragmenting attention—journalists must adapt by making stories more signal-rich and less dependent on sustained focus.

nodes.title: Signal vs. Noise in Modern News
nodes.content: A journalist publishes a well-researched investigative piece on tax evasion. The signal is strong: clear data, expert sources, direct impact on readers. But the noise is also high: social media algorithms suppress links to long articles, readers scroll past long paragraphs, trolls flood comments with irrelevant attacks. To fight noise, the journalist creates a short video summary (increases signal clarity), writes a compelling summary tweet (fits the channel), and provides downloadable data sheets (gives evidence in accessible form). By understanding channel capacity, journalists realize they can't just publish good work—they must actively reduce noise interference to ensure their signal reaches the audience intact.

nodes.title: Compression & Redundancy in Reporting
nodes.content: Redundancy occurs when information is repeated unnecessarily. In information theory, compression removes this redundancy to transmit the same content with fewer bits. For journalists, redundancy appears in repetitive phrases, restated facts, and verbose explanations. Effective compression means saying more with less: using active voice, removing adjectives that repeat meaning, consolidating sources. A story compressed by 30% through editing loses no essential information but gains clarity and readability. However, some redundancy is valuable—repetition reinforces key points for audiences skimming headlines. The skill is distinguishing harmful redundancy (that obscures) from helpful redundancy (that clarifies). Mastering compression makes you a better editor and reporter.

nodes.title: Compression in Action: Before and After
nodes.content: Original (redundant): 'The mayor, speaking today in a press conference that took place today, said that he said the city's new transit plan, which is a plan for improving transit, is important and is a priority. The plan is expected to improve transit.' (47 words, repetitive). Compressed: 'The mayor called the city's new transit plan a priority, saying it will improve transit.' (15 words, same information). Notice how compression removes 'speaking today in a press conference that took place today' (redundant phrasing), 'which is a plan for improving transit' (the word 'plan' already implies this), and the repetition of 'important and is a priority' and 'improve transit.' This example shows how information theory principles help journalists write more efficiently—the compressed version has the same signal with less noise.

nodes.title: Synthesis: Integrated Information Theory in Journalism
nodes.content: You've explored three dimensions of information theory. Now integrate them: (1) Entropy teaches you to lead with surprising, high-information facts that make stories compelling. (2) Channel capacity reminds you that clarity, evidence, and removing noise are as important as the story itself—format matters. (3) Compression shows you that efficiency and precision aren't the enemy of storytelling; they're the foundation. Together, these principles create a framework for impactful journalism: identify high-entropy elements (the unexpected news angle), transmit your signal clearly (strong lead, clear language, accessible format), and compress ruthlessly (remove redundancy, tighten prose). Apply this framework to your next story—you'll notice how information theory transforms both writing and strategy.

nodes.title: You've Mastered Information Theory for Journalism
nodes.content: You now understand how entropy, channel capacity, and compression shape effective communication. These aren't abstract mathematical concepts—they're tools that explain why some stories go viral, how to cut through information overload, and what separates memorable reporting from forgettable filler. As you continue your journalism practice, return to these principles: make your stories surprising and information-rich (entropy), craft them for your audience's actual constraints (channel capacity), and edit with ruthless efficiency (compression). Information theory isn't just for engineers—it's a journalist's superpower.
